title: Back Titration and Conductometric Titration
subtitle: Analysing what you can't titrate directly
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: conduct practical investigations to analyse the concentration of an unknown acid or base by titration
inquiryQuestion: How are solutions of acids and bases analysed?
lessonIntent: Students can run a back titration calculation for an insoluble analyte and interpret a conductometric titration curve.
examSkill: Find moles reacted by subtracting the excess from the total, apply the correct mole ratio, and locate the conductometric endpoint at the conductance minimum.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today, back titration and conductometric titration. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Back Titration and Conductometric Titration (Year 12 Chemistry, Module 6, Lesson 18)

scenes.heading: You can't titrate a chalky tablet directly
scenes.body: Calcium carbonate is barely soluble and reacts slowly, so a direct titration never gives a clean endpoint.
scenes.callout: When direct titration fails, work backwards.
scenes.caption: Some analytes can't be titrated directly; back titration measures them indirectly.

voiceover.text: OK, imagine you need to check how much calcium carbonate is really in an antacid tablet. You can't just drop it in sodium hydroxide and titrate, because carbonate doesn't react with a base. And you can't cleanly titrate it with acid directly either, because calcium carbonate is barely soluble and reacts slowly, fizzing away for minutes without ever giving a sharp endpoint. Regulators have actually found antacids that fell short of their label when tested by direct titration. The fix is to work backwards. Add a known excess of acid, let it fully react, then titrate whatever acid is left over. From that you back out how much reacted with the tablet. That's a back titration, and it's the first half of today's lesson.

scenes.heading: Back titration: react, then measure the leftover
scenes.body: Add a known excess of reagent, let it react fully, then titrate the unreacted excess.

nodes.label: n reacted = n total − n excess

bullets.text: Use it when the analyte is insoluble, slow to react, or has no clear endpoint.
n(reacted) = n(total reagent added) − n(excess left over).
Then apply the mole ratio to find the analyte.

scenes.secondary: Always subtract moles, not volumes. The excess can never exceed the total, so a negative answer means a slip.
scenes.caption: Back titration: excess reagent → react → titrate leftover → subtract → ratio.

voiceover.text: So here's the logic of a back titration. You reach for it whenever a direct titration won't work, when the analyte is insoluble, when it reacts too slowly, or when there's just no clear endpoint. The procedure is, add a precisely known excess of standard reagent, usually acid, and let it react completely with the analyte, giving it all the time it needs. Then titrate the acid that's left over with a standard base. Now the key step. The moles that actually reacted with the analyte equal the total moles you added minus the excess moles you found by titration. Subtract moles, never volumes. And a useful sanity check, the excess can never be more than the total you added, so if your subtraction goes negative, you've mixed up which is which. Once you have the moles reacted, the balanced equation's mole ratio gives you the analyte.

scenes.heading: Back titration vocabulary

bullets.text: Back titration: titrating the unreacted excess of an added reagent.
Excess reagent: the known surplus added to drive the reaction to completion.
n(reacted): total moles added minus the excess found by titration.
Conductometric titration: tracking conductance to find the endpoint.

scenes.callout: The excess guarantees a complete reaction.
scenes.caption: Back titration, excess reagent, moles reacted, and conductometric titration.

voiceover.text: Quick vocabulary. A back titration is one where you titrate the unreacted excess of a reagent you added, rather than the analyte itself. The excess reagent is the deliberate surplus you add to make sure the reaction with the analyte goes all the way to completion. The moles reacted is the total moles you added minus the excess you measure by titration, and that subtraction is the heart of the method. And conductometric titration, the second technique today, finds the endpoint by tracking the solution's electrical conductance as you add titrant, instead of watching for a colour change. Both methods exist for the same reason, to handle situations where a plain indicator titration just won't do.

scenes.heading: Percentage CaCO₃ in an antacid
scenes.question: A crushed antacid tablet (mass 620 mg) is treated with 25.00 mL of 0.500 mol L⁻¹ HCl. The excess HCl needs 18.40 mL of 0.250 mol L⁻¹ NaOH. CaCO₃ + 2HCl → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂. Find the mass and % of CaCO₃.
scenes.coachNote: Watch the 1:2 ratio, then divide the reacted HCl by 2.
scenes.caption: Reacted HCl 7.90 × 10⁻³ mol; CaCO₃ = 395 mg = 63.8% of the tablet.

voiceover.text: Let's do the classic back titration. A crushed antacid tablet weighing six hundred and twenty milligrams is treated with twenty-five millilitres of nought-point-five mole per litre hydrochloric acid. The leftover acid needs eighteen-point-four millilitres of nought-point-two-five mole per litre sodium hydroxide. Step one, the total acid added. Nought-point-five times nought-point-zero-two-five is one-point-two-five times ten to the minus two moles. Step two, the excess acid, from the back titration. Nought-point-two-five times nought-point-zero-one-eight-four is four-point-six times ten to the minus three. Step three, subtract. The acid that reacted with the carbonate is one-point-two-five times ten to the minus two minus four-point-six times ten to the minus three, which is seven-point-nine times ten to the minus three moles. Step four, the crucial ratio. Calcium carbonate reacts with two acids, so divide by two, giving three-point-nine-five times ten to the minus three moles of carbonate. Times the molar mass of about a hundred, that's three hundred and ninety-five milligrams, which is sixty-three-point-eight percent of the tablet. The tablet's a bit short of its label, and forgetting to divide by two would have doubled that figure.

scenes.heading: Conductometric titration: watch the ions
scenes.body: Conductance falls as fast H⁺ is replaced by slow Na⁺, hits a minimum at the equivalence point, then rises.

bullets.text: H⁺ conducts best (350), then OH⁻ (198), Cl⁻ (76), Na⁺ (50).
Before EP: H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O swaps fast H⁺ for slow Na⁺, so conductance falls.
After EP: excess OH⁻ is added, so conductance rises again.

scenes.secondary: The equivalence point is the minimum of the V-shape, found where the two straight lines cross. It works even for coloured solutions.
scenes.callout: EP is the minimum, not the maximum.
scenes.caption: SA+SB conductance: V-shape, minimum at the EP as H⁺ (350) → Na⁺ (50).

voiceover.text: Now the second technique, conductometric titration, which finds the endpoint without any indicator by tracking how well the solution conducts electricity. The key fact is that different ions conduct very differently. The hydrogen ion is the best conductor by far, then hydroxide, then chloride, then sodium. So take a strong acid being titrated with a strong base. At the start, the solution is full of fast hydrogen ions, so conductance is high. As you add base, hydrogen ions and hydroxide combine into water, and each highly conducting hydrogen ion is effectively replaced by a sluggish sodium ion. So the conductance drops, steadily, all the way down. It bottoms out at the equivalence point, when all the hydrogen ions are gone and only sodium and chloride remain. After that, you're adding excess hydroxide, a good conductor, so the conductance climbs again. The result is a V-shape, and the equivalence point is the minimum, where the two straight lines meet. And because it reads ions, not colour, it works even on coloured or cloudy solutions where an indicator would be useless.

scenes.heading: Three traps across both methods
scenes.body: Trap 1: using a 1:1 ratio for CaCO₃. It reacts with two acids, so divide by 2.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: subtracting volumes instead of moles in the back calculation. Trap 3: calling the conductometric endpoint the maximum; for strong acid with strong base it's the minimum.
scenes.callout: CaCO₃ is 1:2, subtract moles, EP is the minimum.
scenes.caption: CaCO₃ needs ÷2; subtract moles not volumes; conductometric EP is the minimum.

voiceover.text: Three traps spanning both methods. Trap one, using a one to one ratio for calcium carbonate. It reacts with two acids, not one, so you must divide the reacted acid by two. Dropping that factor of two is the single most common back titration error, and it doubles your reported percentage. Trap two, subtracting volumes instead of moles. The whole method rests on moles reacted equals total moles minus excess moles, so always convert to moles before you subtract. Trap three, on the conductometric side, calling the endpoint the maximum conductance. For a strong acid with a strong base, the endpoint is the minimum, the bottom of the V, where all the fast hydrogen ions have been consumed. Three small but costly slips, all easy to avoid once you've named them.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: An antacid (containing CaCO₃) is dissolved in 50.00 mL of 1.00 mol L⁻¹ HCl (excess). The unreacted HCl needs 28.00 mL of 0.500 mol L⁻¹ NaOH. How many moles of HCl reacted with the CaCO₃?
answerSteps: So 0.036 mol of HCl reacted with the CaCO₃ ✓
scenes.caption: 0.0500 − 0.0140 = 0.0360 mol HCl reacted with the CaCO₃.

voiceover.text: Quick check, straight back titration arithmetic. An antacid containing calcium carbonate is dissolved in fifty millilitres of one mole per litre hydrochloric acid, which is in excess. The unreacted acid then needs twenty-eight millilitres of nought-point-five mole per litre sodium hydroxide. How many moles of acid reacted with the carbonate? Pause and subtract the moles. Right, let's check. The total acid added is one times nought-point-zero-five, which is nought-point-zero-five moles. The excess, from the back titration, is nought-point-five times nought-point-zero-two-eight, which is nought-point-zero-one-four moles. So the acid that reacted with the carbonate is nought-point-zero-five minus nought-point-zero-one-four, which is nought-point-zero-three-six moles. And if you went one step further to the carbonate itself, you'd divide by two for the one to two ratio, giving nought-point-zero-one-eight moles. Subtract the moles, then remember the ratio.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: Back titration suits insoluble, slow, or endpoint-free analytes.
n(reacted) = n(total added) − n(excess); subtract moles, not volumes.
CaCO₃ reacts 1:2 with HCl, so always divide the reacted acid by 2.
Conductometric titration finds the endpoint from conductance, no indicator.
Strong acid with strong base: conductance V-shape, minimum at the EP.
scenes.finalPrompt: Insoluble or coloured analyte? Reach for back or conductometric titration.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: when to back titrate, the subtraction, the ratio, conductance.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, back titration is the method for analytes that are insoluble, react too slowly, or give no clear endpoint. Second, the moles that reacted equal the total moles added minus the excess found by titration, and you always subtract moles, never volumes. Third, calcium carbonate reacts with two acids per formula, so you must divide the reacted acid by two. Fourth, conductometric titration finds the endpoint by tracking conductance, so it needs no indicator and works on coloured or cloudy solutions. And fifth, for a strong acid with a strong base, the conductance traces a V-shape, with the equivalence point at the minimum. When a sample is insoluble or coloured, reach for back titration or the conductometric method. See you in the next lesson.
